   – Aggregated deal databases (PitchBook, Crunchbase, CB Insights) for deal count by HQ location.

   – 95th percentile (~0.70): only if US share rebounds strongly toward historical peaks.

<parameter_estimation_process>
1. Parameter: Estimating the population mean (μ) of total funding raised (in millions USD) by venture-backed companies with up to 8 employees.
2. Known facts/data:
   • Seed rounds: median ≈ $2 M, mean ≈ $3 M  
   • Pre-seed rounds: median ≈ $1 M  
   • Series A rounds: median ≈ $9 M, mean ≈ $12 M  
   • Companies with ≤ 8 employees are often at pre-seed, seed, or early Series A.
3. Possible data sources/methods:
   • Crunchbase, PitchBook, CB Insights datasets  
   • NVCA annual reports  
   • Publicly filed round data, surveys of accelerators/incubators  
   • Direct sampling of startup registries
4. Influencing factors:
   • Stage distribution (pre-seed vs seed vs A)  
   • Geography (US vs EU vs APAC)  
   • Industry sector capital intensity  
   • Market cycle (bear vs bull funding climates)
5. Potential biases/limitations:
   • Survivorship bias (successful startups more likely to report)  
   • Self-reporting errors, incomplete round data  
   • Skew from a few highly funded outliers
6. Assumptions:
   • Data reflect a roughly stable 3-year window of rounds  
   • Inclusion of all disclosed rounds for companies ≤ 8 employees  
   • No major structural shift in fundraising norms
7. Temporal/subgroup changes:
   • Funding sizes have generally trended up over the past decade  
   • Regional differences: US West Coast rounds larger on average  
   • Tech-heavy sectors may skew high relative to non-tech
8. Quantile estimates (population mean μ):
   • 5th percentile: 1.5 M  
   • 25th percentile: 3.0 M  
   • 50th percentile (median): 5.0 M  
   • 75th percentile: 8.0 M  
   • 95th percentile: 12.0 M
9. Relevant context:
   • Typical small‐team startups raise between $1 M and $10 M in early rounds  
   • Few remain under 8 employees by later rounds, biasing average upward
10. Justification:
   • Lower tail (pre-seed heavy) pushes mean down toward $1–3 M  
   • Middle of distribution driven by seed and small Series A rounds (~$5 M)  
   • Upper tail includes a minority of high-velocity startups raising $10–15 M
11. Emphasis on parameter uncertainty:
   • These quantiles express my credence about μ itself, not individual round sizes.
12. Spread interpretation:
   • A relatively wide 5th–95th range (1.5–12 M) indicates moderate uncertainty about true mean.
13. Edge cases/alternatives:
   • If data were dominated by pre-seed only, μ could be below $3 M (lower tail).  
   • If most companies rapidly scaled to A-round, μ could exceed $8 M (upper tail).
</parameter_estimation_process>

<q5>1.5</q5>
<q25>3.0</q25>
<q50>5.0</q50>
<q75>8.0</q75>
<q95>12.0</q95>

<justification>
Small venture-backed teams typically raise between $1 M and $10 M in aggregate. Pre-seed and seed‐stage companies (with ≤ 8 employees) anchor the lower tail (~$1–3 M), seed and early Series A set the central tendency (~$5 M), and a few high-fliers raise $10 M+ pulling the mean upward. The chosen percentiles reflect these stage distributions, outlier influence, and modest data uncertainties.
</justification>

<confidence_level>
Moderate—while seed-stage typical ranges are well documented, stage mix, regional variations, and outliers introduce uncertainty, hence a fairly wide credible interval for the population mean.
</confidence_level>

   – Reporting bias: smaller raises may go unreported.  
   – Temporal shifts: funding amounts trended upward over the last decade.

   – The population of interest is roughly similar across sectors/geographies or weighted toward tech hubs.  
   – Funding data is log-normal but transformed through our belief into a roughly symmetric uncertainty around 12M.  
   – No catastrophic market crash or boom skewing all rounds drastically.

   – 50th percentile: 12M – central estimate, aligns with median Series A plus seed.  
   – 75th percentile: 16M – sectors/geographies with robust funding (e.g., biotech or major tech hubs).  
   – 95th percentile: 24M – high-funding outlier ecosystems or unusually large early raises.

9. Relevant context  
   – Startups with ~15 employees are often post-Series A or in late-seed/Series B.  
   – Typical total raised reflects cumulative seed + A, sometimes early B.

10. Justification of choices  
    – Grounded in publicly reported round sizes.  
    – Spread reflects moderate uncertainty about market conditions and reporting biases.

    – These quantiles describe uncertainty about the true mean μ, not the distribution of individual raises.

12. What the spread indicates

12. Reflection on estimate spread
   - Moderate spread (60 vs. 350) reflects heavy tails and limited precise data.

   - A cohort dominated by small Series B companies could pull μ closer to $50 M.
